summary: I am from Michigan and grew up in the metropolitan Detroit area. I attended the University of Michigan for both my undergraduate degree and my master's in library science. Michigan was where I first discovered my passion for languages and later library science. It was the foundation of my education and early career decisions.

Detroit is where I grew up and later returned to work after my first job in Chicago. I worked in hospital libraries there, including the prestigious Harper Hospital Library at the Detroit Medical Center. Detroit was central to my career development in hospital librarianship during the 1970s and 1980s.

I attended the University of Michigan for both my undergraduate studies and my master's degree in library science. It was there that I took Latin and Ancient Greek, which later helped me in my medical librarian career. The university provided me with the academic foundation and the library science program that shaped my career path.

In 2004, I moved my family to North Carolina to take a job at Wake Forest University Library. This was a big transition from Michigan, but it was an important step in my career. At Wake Forest, I continued my work in library administration and was introduced to the Searle group, which influenced my professional network.

After living and working in Michigan for many years, I relocated to North Carolina in 2004 for a library position at Wake Forest University. I now live in Beaufort, North Carolina, which I consider heaven and enjoy very much. This move marked a significant change in my personal and professional life.

I currently live in Beaufort, North Carolina, and I really enjoy it here. After retiring five years ago, this place has felt like heaven to me. It's a peaceful and pleasant place to settle down after a long career in librarianship.

I worked at Wayne State University in Detroit where I earned my PhD in education while working in the library system. I started as a science librarian and eventually became director of an undergraduate library, which opened in 1997. Wayne State was a major part of my career and professional growth in academic librarianship.

I studied Latin in high school and continued it as part of my college language requirement at the University of Michigan. Latin was very helpful in my early career, especially working in a hospital library in Chicago, because it helped me understand medical terminology. It was one of the practical benefits of my classical studies background.

I majored in classical Greek at the University of Michigan because I loved the language, even though it was not practical. My knowledge of Ancient Greek, along with Latin, enriched my understanding and was surprisingly useful in my medical librarian career. It was part of my academic passion before moving into library science.

My first job after library school was in a hospital library in Chicago. I was 22 years old and eager to gain big city experience, and Chicago was the perfect place for that. Although I only stayed 14 months, it was an important early step in my career and helped me apply my Latin knowledge to medical terminology.

Right after graduation, I attended the Medical Library Association conference where I interviewed for my first job. Later, I admired a hospital librarian from Harper Hospital who became president of the Medical Library Association, which was rare and inspiring. The association and its network were important throughout my hospital librarian career.

Harper Hospital was where I landed my dream job as head of the hospital library in the Detroit Medical Center. It was a large and prestigious hospital library with nine staff members, unlike most tiny hospital libraries. I worked there during the 1970s and early 1980s, a time of major healthcare downsizing that deeply affected our library staff.

The Detroit Medical Center housed Harper Hospital, where I was head of the hospital library. It was a significant part of my career in the 70s and 80s, especially as the healthcare system underwent harsh transformations. Working there gave me experience managing a larger hospital library and navigating difficult institutional changes.

At Wayne State University Library, I transitioned from hospital librarianship to academic librarianship as a science librarian. I later became director of the undergraduate library, which I designed and opened in 1997 to better serve undergraduates. This role was a highlight of my career and led me into library administration.

I applied for the director of the library at Oakland University in Rochester, Michigan, but was not selected because the other finalist had a PhD. This motivated me to pursue my own PhD at Wayne State University while balancing work and family. Oakland University was a turning point that pushed me to further my education.

Rochester, Michigan is where Oakland University is located, where I applied for a library director position. Although I was not selected for the job, the experience inspired me to earn a PhD to strengthen my qualifications. Rochester was thus connected to an important decision in my academic and professional life.

My husband worked for an online bookseller covering the Research Triangle area in North Carolina, which included Wake Forest University. His visits there and interest in the area eventually led to my job opportunity at Wake Forest in 2004. The Research Triangle indirectly influenced our family's move south.

Searle was a professional group I joined soon after starting at Wake Forest University. It shared a resource-sharing ethos that resonated with my background as a hospital librarian. John Berger reached out to me early on, and although I was shy, I found the network valuable for my work and professional connections.

John Berger was one of the first people to contact me when I joined the Searle group at Wake Forest University. He reached out by call or email to welcome me and introduce me to the group. His friendly approach helped me feel comfortable in the professional network during my early days there.

I attended meetings of the American Chemical Society as part of my professional activities, especially in connection with Searle and my role as a science librarian. These meetings were part of the broader network and resources that supported my work. They helped me stay connected with developments in scientific information sharing.
